tossups.question: The Inuit statesman Tagak Curley received an apology from this author’s great-grandson for “150 years” of pain caused by a play he edited. In Culture and Imperialism, Edward Said used the belief of a character created by this author that “the earth was made for them to trade in, and the sun and moon was made to give them light” to illustrate the “overweening self-importance” created by imperial markets. This author heavily edited the play The (*) Frozen Deep for Wilkie Collins, specifically to shift blame for the Franklin Expedition to the Inuit. This author’s distaste for George Catlin caused this author to write a racist essay sarcastically titled “The Noble Savage.” The fictional Borrioboola-Gha people benefit from the “telescopic philanthropy” of a character created by this author. This (*) author’s observations on slavery in the travelogue American Notes provided materials for a novel by this creator of Ms. Jellyby whose title character is apprenticed to Seth Pecksniff. For 10 points, name this author, who often centrally featured colonial trading firms in novels like Dombey and Son.
tossups.answer: Charles Dickens [prompt on Wilkie Collins until mentioned with “who worked on Frozen Deep with what other author as editor?”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Charles Dickens [prompt on Wilkie Collins until mentioned with "who worked on Frozen Deep with what other author as editor?"]

The fictional Borrioboola-Gha people benefit from the "telescopic philanthropy" of a character created by this author. For 10 points, name this author, who often centrally featured colonial trading firms in novels like Dombey and Son.

tossups.question: Because of a laudanum addiction, an author with this surname believed that he was always accompanied by a doppelgӓnger whom he called a “ghost” version of himself. A character with this surname draws gasps when he willingly chooses to read Fordyce’s Sermons after teatime. That character with this surname absurdly lists his reasons for proposing to his cousin, who promptly tells him that she is the “last woman in the world” who could make him happy. This is the surname of an obsequious clergyman who allies with Lady Catherine de Bourgh in Pride and Prejudice. An author with this surname wrote a detective novel in which Godfrey Ablewhite is revealed to be the one who stole the title diamond from Rachel Verinder. For 10 points, identify this surname of the author of The Moonstone, Wilkie.

packet.name: G (Cambridge A, Carleton College A, and Penn A)

tossups.answer_sanitized: Collins [accept William Collins or William Wilkie Collins]
tossups.question_sanitized: Because of a laudanum addiction, an author with this surname believed that he was always accompanied by a doppelgаnger whom he called a "ghost" version of himself. A character with this surname draws gasps when he willingly chooses to read Fordyce's Sermons after teatime. That character with this surname absurdly lists his reasons for proposing to his cousin, who promptly tells him that she is the "last woman in the world" who could make him happy. This is the surname of an obsequious clergyman who allies with Lady Catherine de Bourgh in Pride and Prejudice. An author with this surname wrote a detective novel in which Godfrey Ablewhite is revealed to be the one who stole the title diamond from Rachel Verinder. For 10 points, identify this surname of the author of The Moonstone, Wilkie.

tossups.question: A character created by this author frequently whistles “The Last Rose of Summer” to himself and is assisted by a boy with bulging eyes nicknamed “Gooseberry.” An actress in a novel by this author eventually marries Captain Kirke after wooing Frank Clare and discovering that her will was invalidated when her father died in a train crash. Another character in a novel by him catches typhus while lying on a roof in the rain to overhear the plot of (*) Percival, who intends to pay his debt using money gained from a marriage to her half-sister Laura Fairlie. This author of No Name wrote a novel in which Sergeant Cuff attempts to find the title object, which is stolen from Rachel Verinder by Godfrey Ablewhite. For 10 points, name this author of The Woman in White and The Moonstone.
tossups.question_sanitized: A character created by this author frequently whistles "The Last Rose of Summer" to himself and is assisted by a boy with bulging eyes nicknamed "Gooseberry." This author of No Name wrote a novel in which Sergeant Cuff attempts to find the title object, which is stolen from Rachel Verinder by Godfrey Ablewhite. For 10 points, name this author of The Woman in White and The Moonstone.

tossups.question: One of this author's characters whistles “The Last Rose of Summer” and wants to grow roses when he retires. In another of this author’s novels, the obese Count Fosco betrays and is murdered by an Italian group called the “Brotherhood.” He wrote a work in which art teacher Walter Hartright discovers Laura Fairlie resembles an asylum escapee named Anne, while in another work Godfrey Ablewhite is murdered after pocketing the title object. This author of Armadale collaborated with his friend Charles Dickens in writing the story “A House to Let.” In one of his novels, the title object is a stolen diamond eventually returned to India. For 10 points, name this British author, who pioneered detective fiction in The Woman in White and The Moonstone.
tossups.question_sanitized: One of this author's characters whistles "The Last Rose of Summer" and wants to grow roses when he retires. For 10 points, name this British author, who pioneered detective fiction in The Woman in White and The Moonstone.

bonuses.leadin: This devotee of mesmerism claimed “I have the perfect conviction that I could magnetize a frying pan,”and hypnotized Madame Augusta de la Rue while possibly having an affair with her. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of The Mystery of Edwin Drood, who side-hustled as a mesmeric healer and the magician “The Unparalleled Necromancer Rhia Rhama Rhoos.”

Dickens met his mistress Nelly Ternan while writing and acting in this play, in which Clara Burnham sees distant events while under a mesmeric trance. This play was based on the failure of the Arctic Franklin expedition.
Dickens wrote The Frozen Deep with this author. In one novel by this author, Franklin Blake recreates the events on a night when he was unknowingly given laudanum to reconstruct a crime.
answers: Wilkie Collins [or William Collins] (The novel is The Moonstone.)
answers_sanitized: Charles Dickens [or Charles John Huffam Dickens]
Wilkie Collins [or William Collins] (The novel is The Moonstone.)

parts: Name this novel in which Clym Yeobright’s return to Egdon Heath leads to his unhappy marriage to Eustacia Vye, who still has feelings for her old boyfriend Damon.
The Return of the Native is set in the fictional Wessex county created by this author of The Mayor of Casterbridge.

In The Return of the Native, Clym returns to Egdon Heath from Paris since he hated his job of selling these objects. In a different novel, a man who unknowingly drank laudanum takes one of these objects during a birthday party.

bonuses.leadin: A story written in this manner ends with Sophonisba deciding to turn the title place into a hospital for sick children. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this manner in which the 1858 story “A House to Let” was produced. This general method also produced a 1901 novel in which the Fourth Dimensionists plot to take over Earth, titled The Inheritors.
This author collaborated with Adelaide Procter and Elizabeth Gaskell on “A House to Let” and published it in his magazine Household Words. He also wrote A Tale of Two Cities and Oliver Twist.

This man was the fourth author of “A House to Let.” He published many of his novels, including an early detective novel about Rachel Verinder’s stolen diamond, in Charles Dickens’s literary magazines.
answers: collaborative fiction [or word forms of collaboration; accept equivalents of being written by a group of or multiple authors; accept chain or mosaic writing] (The Inheritors is by Joseph Conrad and Ford Madox Ford.)
answers_sanitized: collaborative fiction [or word forms of collaboration; accept equivalents of being written by a group of or multiple authors; accept chain or mosaic writing] (The Inheritors is by Joseph Conrad and Ford Madox Ford.)

parts_sanitized: Identify this manner in which the 1858 story "A House to Let" was produced. This general method also produced a 1901 novel in which the Fourth Dimensionists plot to take over Earth, titled The Inheritors.
This author collaborated with Adelaide Procter and Elizabeth Gaskell on "A House to Let" and published it in his magazine Household Words. He also wrote A Tale of Two Cities and Oliver Twist.

This man was the fourth author of "A House to Let." He published many of his novels, including an early detective novel about Rachel Verinder's stolen diamond, in Charles Dickens's literary magazines.

bonuses.leadin: Rosanna Spearman’s suicide note accuses Franklin Blake of this novel’s central crime, although a reenactment confirms that he was innocent and acting under the influence of opium. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel in which Sergeant Cuff determines that Godfrey Ablewhite stole the title precious object.

In “The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone,” this detective swaps places with his wax effigy to recover the title diamond from the thief Count Sylvius. Unusually, that story is not narrated by this man’s companion, Dr. Watson.

This man recovers a stolen emerald necklace hidden between a church’s ornately-carved cherubim (“CHAIR-oo-bim”) in the novel The Nine Tailors. This employer of Mervyn Bunter stars in the novels Whose Body? and Gaudy Night.
answers: Lord Peter Wimsey [or Lord Peter Wimsey] (created by Dorothy Sayers)

packet.name: L - Duke A, Harvard A, Warwick A, Texas A

bonuses.leadin: Answer these questions on Charles Dickens’ social circle. For 10 points each:
parts: Walter Savage Landor, a friend of Dickens, was caricatured by this Bleak House character. An old friend of John Jarndyce, this character spends much of the novel warring with Sir Leicester Dedlock over a garden path.

Dickens enjoyed a close friendship with this author; they were travelling buddies, and likely solicited sex workers together. Dickens serialised this author’s novel, The Woman in White, in his literary magazine All the Year Round.

Edgar Allan Poe fell out with Dickens after this other author, also a friend of Dickens, wrote an article denouncing American poetry. This author’s most famous novel is framed as a puppet show, and the main character works as a governess to Sir Pitt Crawley before marrying his son Rawdon.

bonuses.leadin: This novel’s antagonist boasts about making the prostitute Anna Wetherell miscarry after his horse gets startled and kicks her in the stomach. For 10 points each:
parts: A “Character Chart” assigns a sign of the zodiac to each of twelve characters at the start of what longest-ever Booker-winning novel, set during a gold rush in the town of Hokitika?
Eleanor Catton’s The Luminaries is a contemporary example of the “sensation novel,” a melodramatic genre that reached its peak with this Victorian author’s book The Woman in White.

In perhaps the most the Victorian touch of all in The Luminaries, Anna develops an addiction to this drug. Wilkie Collins was himself addicted to this narcotic, which inspired Coleridge to write “Kubla Khan.”

bonuses.leadin: In the most popular novel in this genre, a woman catches a cold after climbing to a roof in the pouring rain to overhear a plan to kill Laura Fairlie. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this genre of emotion-rousing crime fiction that became a huge fad in Victorian literature after the 1860 publication of The Woman in White.

bonuses.leadin: Name these Victorian authors of novels about fallen women, for 10 points each.
parts: Ex-prostitute Mercy Merrick adopts the identity of the Franco-Prussian War casualty Grace Roseberry in The New Mag- 7 dalen, an out-of-print novel by this author who’s better known for The Woman in White.

An orphan sweatshop girl is swept into an afair with the rakish aristocrat Henry Bellingham in this author’s depressing book Ruth, and the title character’s sister Esther becomes a streetwalker in her novel Mary Barton.

This author wrote a novel in which the kitchen-maid Esther is impregnated by the footman William Latch, but resolves to raise the child despite facing hardships such as her family moving to Australia.

answers_sanitized: George Moore [the book is Esther Waters]

bonuses.leadin: The title character of this novel is Anne Catherick, a mentally ill woman who dies of a heart condition, after which Laura Fairlie takes her place in the asylum. For 10 points each:.
parts: Name this novel which ends with the marriage of Laura, who escapes the asylum with the help of Marian, and the art teacher Walter Hartwright, who blackmails Count Fosco into restoring Laura’s identity.
The Woman in White was written by this British author, who collaborated with Charles Dickens on No Thoroughfare. He also wrote of Rachel Verinder’s inheritance of the title object in The Moonstone.

Following The Woman in White, Collins published this novel, in which Magdalen and Norah Vanstone lose their inheritance to their older brother Michael because their parents were married for only a few months.

bonuses.leadin: Examples of this literary genre include Mrs. Henry Wood’s novel East Lynne and Mary Elizabeth Braddon’s novel Lady Audley’s Secret. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this popular genre of novels in Victorian England, which focused on the extreme mental states caused by crimes and illicit passions.
This author created a mania for sensation novels by writing The Woman in White and The Moonstone.

This author was known as the Irish Wilkie Collins, although he denied that he was a sensation novelist in the “preliminary word” to his novel Uncle Silas. His collection In a Glass Darkly includes his novella about lesbian vampires, Carmilla.
